name: Signal Lens
tagline: News tuned to your life, not your feed.
description: Signal Lens is a personalized news app that turns overwhelming headline streams into context-rich, decision-ready updates based on what is actually affecting your day. It watches for meaningful signals around your location, schedule, subscriptions, interests, and upcoming plans, then explains why a story matters now instead of just showing what is trending. For example, if a subway line you use is delayed, it surfaces the transit article, maps the impact, and suggests alternatives before you leave home. If you have a flight tomorrow, it highlights weather, airport changes, and labor disruptions that could affect your trip, all in one concise briefing. The app solves the problem of news fatigue by filtering out noise and prioritizing actionable information users can use immediately. What makes it unique is that it blends local context, calendar awareness, and plain-language impact summaries so news becomes a practical daily tool rather than a passive stream.
features: Impact-first briefings explain each story in terms of how it affects your commute, money, health, or plans, so users can understand relevance at a glance.
Calendar-aware alerts connect news events to upcoming meetings, travel, and personal commitments, helping users avoid surprises before they happen.
Location-triggered updates surface hyperlocal developments only when they matter nearby, which keeps the app useful without becoming noisy.
Plain-language context cards summarize the who, what, why, and likely consequences of complex stories, making it easier to follow breaking news quickly.
Action suggestions recommend practical next steps, such as changing a route, rescheduling an appointment, or checking a policy update, so news leads to action.
audience: Busy professionals, commuters, travelers, and urban residents aged 22–55 who want news that is relevant to their schedules and everyday decisions. It is especially useful for people who feel overloaded by general news apps but still need timely awareness.
useCases: Sarah just finished a stressful meeting and wants to know whether her evening train home will be affected, so she opens the app and sees a disruption summary with the best alternate route.
Diego has a flight in the morning and checks the app over breakfast to see if weather, airport delays, or airline labor news might impact his departure time.
Mina is deciding whether to bring her kids downtown for a weekend event and opens the app to see nearby protest updates, road closures, and local safety notices in one quick briefing.
